items.title: Toronto mother found not criminally responsible after infant son's 2024 death
items.summary_auto: A 30-year-old woman has been found not criminally responsible for the 2024 death of her infant son in midtown Toronto.

items.title: Why the Lebanon-Israel ceasefire is 'in name only'
items.summary_auto: Nearly three weeks into a U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel, escalating attacks and widening incursions are exposing major cracks in an agreement that analysts say was never going to ultimately halt the violence.

items.title: Former NHL goalie Carey Price among those calling for changes to Indian Act
items.summary_auto: More witnesses weighed in Tuesday on legislation that would make changes to registration under the Indian Act, including eliminating the second-generation cut-off.

items.title: Carney makes aerospace announcement near Montreal
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney participates in an event announcing an aerospace investment in Mirabel, Que.

items.title: Russia fires dozens of drones at Ukraine in 'obvious spurning' of ceasefire, says Zelenskyy
items.summary_auto: Russia fired dozens of drones at Ukraine in nighttime attacks, Ukrainian officials said Wednesday, disregarding a unilateral ceasefire announced by Kyiv that began at midnight.

items.title: Calgary criminal defence lawyer charged with 9 offences, including child luring
items.summary_auto: Criminal defence lawyer Jordan Bonner has been charged with child luring and sexual exploitation offences, six months after he was charged with harassing a police officer.

items.title: Israel strikes Beirut suburbs for 1st time since ceasefire was announced
items.summary_auto: Israel struck Beirut on Wednesday for the first time since agreeing to a ceasefire with Hezbollah last month, with Israel saying it targeted a commander of the militant group's elite Radwan force in the city's southern suburbs.

items.title: How will FIFA World Cup events affect Vancouver? Officials give update on impacts
Involuntary addiction treatment now legal in Sask. despite medical groups' warnings
items.summary_auto: The Compassionate Intervention Act is expected to come into force this fall after regulations are finalized. People forced into treatment will be taken to Saskatchewan Hospital, a psychiatric facility in North Battleford.

items.title: Michelin releases 2026 guide for Quebec, awards stars to 4 new restaurants
items.summary_auto: The second-ever Michelin guide for Quebec is out and four new restaurants have been awarded prestigious stars.

items.title: Ted Turner, founder of CNN, dead at 87
items.summary_auto: Ted Turner, ​the brash sportsman and entrepreneur whose ambition and instincts led to a media empire that included groundbreaking news network CNN, has died, CNN reported on Wednesday citing a press release from Turner Enterprises. He was 87.

items.title: Fired B.C. teacher wins job back after pro-Freedom Convoy presentation
items.summary_auto: On Jan. 28, 2022, Richmond, B.C., teacher Nicole Kowal-Seafoot told a colleague before a presentation that, "I might be losing my job today." More than four years after she did lose her job, for a presentation that was in favour of the self-styled "Freedom Convoy," a labour arbitrator has ruled that the Richmond School District's decision was excessive.

items.title: Markets soar, oil prices plummet as hopes rise for U.S.-Iran peace
items.summary_auto: Oil prices briefly fell under $100 US on Wednesday on hopes that Iran and the U.S. could make a peace deal, before rising a bit again. Wall Street and international markets all soared following the news.

items.title: Canadian sues U.S. Homeland Security, which allegedly sought his Google data after critical social media posts
items.summary_auto: A Canadian is fighting back in U.S. federal court over what he says is an attempt by the Department of Homeland Security, through Google, to seek "vast swaths of information" about his personal life following social media posts critical of Donald Trump's administration.

items.title: Northern Alberta municipality wants province to cover cost of rescuing hundreds of drivers stranded on highway in snowstorm
items.summary_auto: Mayor Sandy Bowman wants to bill Alberta after his municipality rescued hundreds of people stranded on Highway 63 during a recent snowstorm.

items.title: Seeding delayed in parts of Saskatchewan due to flooding
items.summary_auto: John Zbitniff says this is the week he'd normally be out in his fields seeding. But not this year. His land in the RM of Good Lake in east-central Saskatchewan is covered in water.

items.title: WHO confirms Andes strain of hantavirus in cruise ship passengers, with 3 transferred from ship for treatment
items.summary_auto: Health authorities have identified the Andes strain of hantavirus, which can be transmitted from person to person in passengers who were on a cruise ship at the centre of a deadly outbreak of the rare infection, officials said Wednesday.

items.title: Call for more addiction treatments in northern Ontario as overdose deaths reach 'unprecedented' level
items.summary_auto: More treatments for addictions are needed as northern Ontario communities face a spike in overdoses tied to an increasingly toxic drug supply, according to front-line workers, physicians and addiction experts.

items.title: Farm irrigation systems aren't cheap. For N.S. growers, going without could be costlier
items.summary_auto: After a harsh growing season in 2025 due to severe drought, more Nova Scotia farmers are investing in irrigation equipment to ensure their crops can survive extreme weather events.

items.title: I was struggling as a new mother. Finding my mom group made me realize I wasn't alone
items.summary_auto: Emily Wagner was seven months pregnant when her husband fell off a ledge and sustained life-altering injuries. The cumulative trauma of his accident, combined with postpartum depression, left her feeling alone. What saved her was the community she found through her mom group.

items.title: As drinking dries up, Quebec's liquor board faces criticism over efforts to boost sales
items.summary_auto: With the consumption of alcohol on a steady decline, the province’s state-run liquor retailer is making an aggressive attempt to make sure sales don’t dip. Public health experts question whether it's going too far.

items.title: Quick-service restaurants are taking a bigger hit as Canadians feel crunched by the cost of living
items.summary_auto: While restaurants of all kinds are feeling pinched, quick-service restaurants are getting hit harder than higher-end ones, according to a report from Restaurants Canada. It's a signal of a K-shaped economy, where those with the most money can still shell out for a nice meal, but those with lower incomes have no option but to cut back.

items.title: Canada to pick between Swedish and U.S. radar planes to protect its skies
items.summary_auto: The government of Canada wants to quickly acquire half a dozen new "flying radars" — officially called Airborne Early Warning and Control (AEW&C) aircraft — at a cost of more than $5 billion.

items.title: Mexico moved extradition goalposts after U.S. indicted top politician over cartel ties: experts
items.summary_auto: The Claudia Sheinbaum administration's demand for "irrefutable" proof before handing over a top elected politician to the U.S. over alleged drug cartel dealings reveals how the Mexican government applies a double standard in its stated battle against organized crime, experts say.

items.title: 'The rats are like a storm': Palestinians seeking refuge in rubble struggle with rodent infestations
items.summary_auto: Palestinians living in tents amid the rubble in the Gaza Strip and aid groups trying to help them say rodent infestations have left them suffering from diseases and unsure how to cope.

items.title: OpenAI didn't respect Canadian privacy law when it trained ChatGPT: investigation
items.summary_auto: OpenAI did not respect Canadian privacy laws when it trained its immensely popular ChatGPT tool, resulting in the collection and use of sensitive personal information, according to a joint investigation.

items.title: Telecom workers say AI being used to monitor employees, disguise accents of offshore agents
items.summary_auto: Telecommunications workers are calling for government restrictions on the use of artificial intelligence in the sector, suggesting the technology is being used to monitor workers and disguise the accents of overseas call centre workers.

items.title: U.S. rights group sues New York Times, claiming discrimination against a white man
items.summary_auto: A U.S. federal civil rights agency filed a discrimination lawsuit Tuesday against the New York Times, claiming that the news organization passed over a white male employee for a promotion in favour of a lesser qualified woman to meet its diversity goals.

items.title: Tumbler Ridge shooting survivor's 5th surgery 'a success,' father says
items.summary_auto: The father of Maya Gebala, the 12-year-old seriously injured during the mass shooting in Tumbler Ridge, B.C., says his daughter has undergone a successful cranioplasty.

items.title: Canada and Europe should get ‘creative’ in forging closer ties, EU ambassador says
items.summary_auto: The EU’s Ambassador to Canada, Geneviève Tuts, says the bloc is looking to "elevate" its relationship with this country and take it to the "next level."

items.title: Carney government 'not working' to enshrine nature targets into law, junior nature minister says
items.summary_auto: Despite the Carney government's parliamentary majority, Canada's junior nature minister said she has no plans to back up recent commitments to protect Canada's waters and lands in legislation.

items.title: Vancouver breaks temperature record last set in 1898 amid hot weather across B.C.
items.summary_auto: Environment Canada says Vancouver's high temperature on Monday reached 23.9 C, easily breaking the record of 22.2 C set in 1898.
